questions.stem: A 5-year-old is admitted with meningococcal meningitis. Which isolation precautions should be initiated immediately, and for how long should they be maintained?

options.text: Contact precautions; maintain until discharge
Droplet precautions; maintain for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy
Airborne precautions in a negative-pressure room; maintain for 48 hours
Standard precautions only; meningococcal meningitis is not transmissible person-to-person

questions.rationale: Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcal meningitis) is transmitted via respiratory droplets (large droplets, short range <3 feet) — not airborne (small droplet nuclei). Droplet precautions are required: surgical mask within 3 feet, private room or cohorting, mask for transport. Duration: droplet precautions are maintained for the first 24 hours after initiation of effective antibiotic therapy (typically ceftriaxone), after which the organism is no longer detectable in respiratory secretions. Close contacts (household members, childcare contacts) should receive prophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.

distractorRationales.a: Contact precautions; maintain until discharge misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing respiratory points to Droplet precautions; maintain for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Airborne precautions in a negative-pressure room; maintain for 48 hours misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing respiratory points to Droplet precautions; maintain for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Standard precautions only; meningococcal meningitis is not transmissible person-to-person misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing respiratory points to Droplet precautions; maintain for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Meningococcal meningitis: droplet precautions (NOT airborne) for first 24 hours of effective antibiotics; close contacts need chemoprophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin).

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Meningococcal meningitis: droplet precautions (NOT airborne) for first 24 hours of effective antibiotics; close contacts need chemoprophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin).
visualRationale.conclusion: Meningococcal meningitis: droplet precautions (NOT airborne) for first 24 hours of effective antibiotics; close contacts need chemoprophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin).

questions.stem: A patient in the PACU states she was awake during her surgery — she recalls the surgeons' conversation, felt pressure on her abdomen but no pain, and could not move or signal anyone. She is trembling, tearful, and says 'It was the most terrifying experience of my life.' Which nursing response and plan of care is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure her that awareness under anesthesia is medically impossible with modern monitoring, and that she was likely dreaming

Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per policy, provide immediate emotional support, and initiate referral for trauma-focused psychological counseling for potential PTSD
Document 'patient reports dream during anesthesia' and notify only the charge nurse
Administer a PRN anxiolytic and reassess her emotional state in 2 hours before taking further action

questions.rationale: Intraoperative awareness with recall (accidental awareness during general anesthesia — AAGA) affects approximately 1-2 per 1,000 general anesthetics. Patients may recall sounds, conversation, touch, pressure, or in severe cases pain and complete paralysis (most traumatizing — the patient is conscious but paralyzed and unable to alert the team). PTSD develops in up to 70% of awareness cases if not addressed. Nursing priorities: (1) BELIEVE the patient — dismissing her account (gaslighting) causes profound additional psychological harm, violates patient dignity, and creates significant legal liability; (2) Validate her distress and provide immediate compassionate support; (3) Document her exact words accurately and objectively; (4) Notify the anesthesia provider — awareness should trigger review of anesthetic depth monitoring (BIS), neuromuscular blockade management, and volatile agent delivery; (5) Notify risk management per institutional policy; (6) Refer promptly for trauma-focused therapy (EMDR or CBT for PTSD). Administering sedatives to suppress the patient's report is not appropriate.

distractorRationales.a: Reassure her that awareness under anesthesia is medically impossible with modern monitoring, and that she was misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care pain points to Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Document 'patient reports dream during anesthesia' and notify only the charge nurse misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care pain points to Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Administer a PRN anxiolytic and reassess her emotional state in 2 hours before taking further action misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care pain points to Validate her experience fully, document her report accurately, notify the anesthesia provider and risk management per, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Intraoperative awareness: BELIEVE and VALIDATE the patient. Document accurately. Notify anesthesia + risk management. Refer for trauma/PTSD counseling. Dismissing the experience causes psychological harm. Affects ~1-2/1,000 GAs.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient receiving a blood transfusion who develops hives, pruritus, and a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F) 20 minutes into the transfusion. What is the FIRST nursing action?

options.text: Slow the transfusion rate and administer diphenhydramine as ordered
Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and blood bank
Complete the transfusion quickly since the reaction appears mild
Obtain a blood culture and administer acetaminophen for the fever

questions.rationale: Any signs of a transfusion reaction—including hives, pruritus, and fever—require immediate cessation of the transfusion. The key concern is that mild symptoms can rapidly progress to severe hemolytic or anaphylactic reactions. The tubing must be disconnected (not just slowed) and the IV kept patent with normal saline using new tubing to prevent infusion of remaining blood product. The provider and blood bank must be notified, and the blood bag and tubing are returned to the blood bank for analysis. Continuing or completing the transfusion risks life-threatening escalation.

distractorRationales.a: Slow the transfusion rate and administer diphenhydramine as ordered misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Complete the transfusion quickly since the reaction appears mild misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Obtain a blood culture and administer acetaminophen for the fever misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to Stop the transfusion immediately, keep IV access open with normal saline, and notify the provider and, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Transfusion reaction protocol: STOP → Saline via new line → Notify provider + blood bank → Return blood bag + tubing → Document. Never slow the rate and continue if a reaction is suspected.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is working at a migrant farmworker health clinic. A patient presents with signs of pesticide exposure: miosis, excessive salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, emesis, and muscle fasciculations. Which class of pesticide is most likely involved and what is the priority antidote?

options.text: Pyrethroid insecticide; administer diphenhydramine for allergic response
Organophosphate or carbamate; administer atropine IV to block muscarinic effects, followed by pralidoxime (2-PAM) for organophosphates
Organochlorine; administer activated charcoal and benzodiazepines
Herbicide (glyphosate); administer IV sodium bicarbonate

questions.rationale: Organophosphate and carbamate pesticides inhibit acetylcholinesterase, causing accumulation of acetylcholine at muscarinic and nicotinic receptors. Muscarinic (DUMBELS): Diarrhea/Defecation, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia/Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation/Sweating. Nicotinic: muscle fasciculations, weakness, paralysis. CNS: seizures, coma. Antidote: (1) Atropine IV (competitive muscarinic antagonist) — dose titrated to dry secretions, not pupil size; (2) Pralidoxime (2-PAM) reactivates cholinesterase if given early (before 'aging') — effective for organophosphates, not carbamates. Migrant farmworkers are at high risk due to occupational pesticide exposure.

distractorRationales.a: Pyrethroid insecticide; administer diphenhydramine for allergic response misses the clinical pattern: Community Health bradycardia points to Organophosphate or carbamate; administer atropine IV to block muscarinic effects, followed by pralidoxime (2-PAM) for organophosphates, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Organochlorine; administer activated charcoal and benzodiazepines misses the clinical pattern: Community Health bradycardia points to Organophosphate or carbamate; administer atropine IV to block muscarinic effects, followed by pralidoxime (2-PAM) for organophosphates, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Herbicide (glyphosate); administer IV sodium bicarbonate misses the clinical pattern: Community Health bradycardia points to Organophosphate or carbamate; administer atropine IV to block muscarinic effects, followed by pralidoxime (2-PAM) for organophosphates, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Organophosphate poisoning (DUMBELS): atropine (blocks muscarinic effects) + pralidoxime/2-PAM (reactivates cholinesterase); high risk in farmworkers; titrate atropine to dry secretions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with Stage III pressure injury to the sacrum has a wound that is 4 cm × 6 cm × 2.5 cm deep with 30% yellow slough, 70% granulation tissue, minimal exudate, and intact surrounding skin. Which wound care plan is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Pack wound tightly with dry gauze and change dressing twice daily
Apply silver sulfadiazine cream and cover with gauze
Debride slough with autolytic method using moisture-retentive dressing; avoid tight packing
Apply wet-to-dry dressing to debride the slough mechanically

questions.rationale: For a wound with granulation tissue and slough: autolytic debridement using a moisture-retentive dressing (hydrocolloid or hydrogel) promotes selective removal of devitalized tissue while protecting new granulation. Tight packing (A) disrupts fragile granulation tissue and causes trauma on removal. Wet-to-dry dressings (D) are non-selective — they remove both slough AND healthy tissue. Silver sulfadiazine (B) is for burn wounds, not chronic pressure injuries. Principles: fill (don't pack) dead space, maintain moist wound environment, and protect periwound skin.

distractorRationales.a: Pack wound tightly with dry gauze and change dressing twice daily misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals wound points to Debride slough with autolytic method using moisture-retentive dressing; avoid tight packing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.b: Apply silver sulfadiazine cream and cover with gauze misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals wound points to Debride slough with autolytic method using moisture-retentive dressing; avoid tight packing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Apply wet-to-dry dressing to debride the slough mechanically misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals wound points to Debride slough with autolytic method using moisture-retentive dressing; avoid tight packing, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Pressure injury with slough: autolytic debridement with moisture-retentive dressing. Never tight-pack wounds — fill dead space loosely.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old patient on a medical unit is found to have a 5 cm × 4 cm sacral wound with full-thickness tissue loss, visible subcutaneous fat, and partial slough coverage of the wound base. No bone, tendon, or muscle is visible. What is the correct NPUAP/EPUAP pressure injury stage and priority nursing intervention?

options.text: Stage 1; increase repositioning frequency and apply a transparent film dressing
Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care specialist, and address nutrition
Stage 4; immediate surgical debridement is required before any wound care intervention
Unstageable; cover with dry gauze until slough completely resolves before determining stage or treatment

questions.rationale: NPUAP/EPUAP Pressure Injury Stages: Stage 1 — non-blanchable erythema, intact skin; Stage 2 — partial thickness dermis loss, shallow open ulcer; Stage 3 — full-thickness skin loss with visible subcutaneous fat, may have slough, but no bone/tendon/muscle visible; Stage 4 — full-thickness with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle; Unstageable — base entirely obscured by non-removable slough or eschar. This wound is Stage 3 (full-thickness, subcutaneous fat visible, partial slough but bone/tendon not exposed). Management: moist wound healing (foam, hydrocolloid, or alginate dressings based on exudate level), reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, pressure redistribution support surface, wound care specialist consultation, nutritional assessment (protein, zinc, vitamin C support wound healing). Dry gauze is contraindicated — desiccates wound bed and impairs healing. Slough does not make a wound automatically unstageable (unstageable requires the base to be completely obscured).

distractorRationales.a: Stage 1; increase repositioning frequency and apply a transparent film dressing misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology wound points to Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Stage 4; immediate surgical debridement is required before any wound care intervention misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology wound points to Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Unstageable; cover with dry gauze until slough completely resolves before determining stage or treatment misses the clinical pattern: Gerontology wound points to Stage 3; apply a moisture-retentive dressing, reposition every 2 hours off the sacrum, consult wound care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Stage 3 pressure injury: full-thickness, subcutaneous fat visible, no bone/tendon. Treat with moist wound dressings, 2-hour repositioning, pressure redistribution, nutrition support, and wound care consult. Dry gauze contraindicated.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: After a patient falls while attempting to ambulate unassisted, the nurse completes an incident report. Which statement about incident reports is correct?

options.text: The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care
The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical record and is not discoverable in litigation
Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon request
The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers

questions.rationale: Incident reports (occurrence reports) are internal quality improvement documents: they are NOT referenced in the patient's medical record. Referencing them in the chart (A, D) makes them discoverable in litigation, eliminating their protection as internal QI documents. The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without mentioning the incident report. In many states, incident reports prepared in anticipation of litigation may be protected from discovery, but only if not referenced in the chart. They are not part of the permanent medical record (C).

distractorRationales.a: The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care misses the clinical pattern: Documentation assessment points to The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon misses the clinical pattern: Documentation assessment points to The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers misses the clinical pattern: Documentation assessment points to The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Incident reports: do NOT reference in the medical record; document clinical facts of the event in the chart only — referencing the incident report makes it legally discoverable.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A pregnant patient at 31 weeks presents with epigastric/RUQ pain, headache, nausea, and malaise. Labs reveal: AST 280 U/L, LDH 950 U/L, platelets 74,000/mm3, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL with schistocytes on smear, and normal creatinine. Which condition do these findings indicate?

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)
HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets)
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
Viral hepatitis with thrombocytopenia

questions.rationale: HELLP syndrome is a variant of severe preeclampsia: Hemolysis (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low Hgb), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST >70 U/L), Low Platelets (<100,000). RUQ/epigastric pain from hepatic capsule distension is classic. Definitive treatment is delivery. AFLP (A) presents with marked coagulopathy (elevated PT/INR, hypoglycemia, jaundice) due to hepatocyte fat infiltration — different mechanism. TTP (C) presents with the pentad including fever and renal failure. Viral hepatitis (D) would not present with schistocytes and would have different epidemiology.

distractorRationales.a: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) misses the clinical pattern: HELLP Syndrome hypoglycemia points to HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) misses the clinical pattern: HELLP Syndrome hypoglycemia points to HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Viral hepatitis with thrombocytopenia misses the clinical pattern: HELLP Syndrome hypoglycemia points to HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets), so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: HELLP = Hemolysis (schistocytes + elevated LDH) + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets; treatment is delivery regardless of gestational age.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient develops respiratory distress 12 hours after total thyroidectomy, with stridor, dysphagia, and progressive anterior neck swelling. SpO2 is 88% on 6 L/min nasal cannula. Which action is the NURSE'S IMMEDIATE PRIORITY?

options.text: Administer calcium gluconate IV and draw ionized calcium level
Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression
Increase oxygen to 15 L/min via non-rebreather and call rapid response
Position the patient in high Fowler's and prepare for emergent intubation

questions.rationale: Post-thyroidectomy respiratory distress with anterior neck swelling indicates expanding hematoma compressing the trachea. This is an airway emergency. Bedside wound opening to evacuate the hematoma is the immediate intervention — wound opening kits should be at the bedside on all post-thyroidectomy patients for this reason. While this is being done, the team prepares for emergent surgical re-exploration. Calcium gluconate (A) treats hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism (tetany, perioral numbness, Trousseau's sign) — not the presenting emergency. NRB mask (C) won't overcome a mechanical airway obstruction.

distractorRationales.a: Administer calcium gluconate IV and draw ionized calcium level misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced airway points to Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Increase oxygen to 15 L/min via non-rebreather and call rapid response misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced airway points to Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Position the patient in high Fowler's and prepare for emergent intubation misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced airway points to Open the surgical wound at the bedside to relieve hematoma compression, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Post-thyroidectomy neck swelling + stridor = hematoma compressing airway → open the surgical wound immediately. Keep wound kit at bedside.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old patient with bipolar disorder refuses psychiatric hospitalization. He has not slept in 5 days, believes he is receiving messages from aliens, and stated he plans to 'make his neighbor pay' for 'spying on him.' Which criterion most legally supports involuntary psychiatric commitment?

options.text: Presence of a psychiatric diagnosis requiring treatment
Refusal of recommended medication
Danger to others based on specific expressed threat
Inability to care for personal hygiene and nutrition

questions.rationale: Involuntary psychiatric commitment (civil commitment) requires that the person poses a danger to self, danger to others, or is gravely disabled (unable to meet basic needs of food, clothing, shelter due to mental illness). This patient's specific threat to 'make his neighbor pay' combined with active psychosis meets the danger-to-others criterion. Having a psychiatric diagnosis alone (A) is not sufficient for commitment. Medication refusal (B) alone does not meet criteria. While grave disability (D) could apply, the most legally defensible criterion here is the specific threat to another person.

distractorRationales.a: Presence of a psychiatric diagnosis requiring treatment misses the clinical pattern: Involuntary Commitment safety points to Danger to others based on specific expressed threat, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.b: Refusal of recommended medication misses the clinical pattern: Involuntary Commitment safety points to Danger to others based on specific expressed threat, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Inability to care for personal hygiene and nutrition misses the clinical pattern: Involuntary Commitment safety points to Danger to others based on specific expressed threat, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Involuntary commitment requires: danger to self, danger to others (specific threat + mental illness), or grave disability — psychiatric diagnosis alone is insufficient.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.
